Almost always bug; requiring explicit expect = "n:m" acts speed bump. practice, "1:n" "n:1" cover production joins. detect_cardinality() confirms relationship development; expect value hard-coded production script. check_cartesian() solves different problem: warns Cartesian product explosion key many duplicates sides. join can violate "1:1" constraint without triggering Cartesian explosion (one extra duplicate enough), "n:m" join can produce massive product join_strict() allow. two functions complement . return value list has_explosion, expansion_factor, predicted total_inner row count, worst_keys data frame naming keys responsible. pipeline gate, (chk$has_explosion) stop(...) catches blow-join allocates memory. 20x factor turns 40-row input 800-row result; arithmetic million-row table fills server’s RAM 2 .m.
code: products <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P2", "P3"),   name = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  line_items <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3", "P3"),   order_id = c(101, 102, 103, 104, 105),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  detect_cardinality(products, line_items, by = "product_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "1:n" #> Right duplicates: 2 key(s) result <- join_strict(   products, line_items,   by = "product_id",   type = "left",   expect = "1:n" ) nrow(result) #> [1] 5 products_bad <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3"),   name = c("Widget", "Widget v2", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_strict(   products_bad, line_items,   by = "product_id",   type = "left",   expect = "1:n" ) #> Error in `join_strict()`: #> ! Cardinality violation: expected "1:n" but found "n:m". #> ℹ Left duplicates: 1, right duplicates: 2. events_a <- data.frame(id = rep(c("E1", "E2"), each = 20), src = "a",                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE) events_b <- data.frame(id = rep(c("E1", "E2"), each = 20), src = "b",                        stringsAsFactors = FALSE)  chk <- check_cartesian(events_a, events_b, by = "id") #> ✖ Cartesian product risk: result will be 20x larger than input #>  #> ── Worst offending keys ── #>  #> "E1": 20 x 20 = 400 rows #> "E2": 20 x 20 = 400 rows chk$expansion_factor #> [1] 20

text: Real pipelines chain joins: orders pick customer attributes, customers pick regions, regions pick targets. final table wrong row count, offending join , checking one hand means re-running pipeline step step. analyze_join_chain() walk one call – takes named list tables list join specifications, runs join_spy() every step, carries intermediate result forward left join, prints per-step summary. literal name "result" refers accumulated output previous steps, chains length need one keyword. Step 1 reports order-side duplicates, expected transaction data joining lookup. real finding step 2, match rate drops: region "R3" exists customer table row regions. kind fact hides months three-join pipeline, individual join “works”. return value list one entry per step, holding table names, columns, full JoinReport, everything programmatic-inspection section applies per step: chain-level gate reduces one vector: production run chain analysis deployment time whenever upstream schema changes. Since performs intermediate left join feed next step, cost comparable running pipeline’s joins , makes pre-flight post-incident tool; per-run gates stay key_check() join_strict().
code: orders_chain <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:6,   customer_id = c("C1", "C2", "C2", "C3", "C4", "C4"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers_chain <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C1", "C2", "C3", "C4"),   region_id = c("R1", "R1", "R2", "R3"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  regions <- data.frame(   region_id = c("R1", "R2"),   region_name = c("North", "South"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) chain <- analyze_join_chain(   tables = list(orders = orders_chain, customers = customers_chain,                 regions = regions),   joins = list(     list(left = "orders", right = "customers", by = "customer_id"),     list(left = "result", right = "regions", by = "region_id")   ) ) #>  #> ── Join Chain Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Step 1: orders + customers ── #>  #> Left: 6 rows #> Right: 4 rows #> Match rate: 100% #> Expected result: 6 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Step 2: result + regions ── #>  #> Left: 6 rows #> Right: 2 rows #> Match rate: 66.7% #> Expected result: 6 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Chain Summary ── #>  #> ! Sampling catches systemic problems (wrong key column, widespread encoding issues, duplicate explosion) fraction runtime. set.seed() ahead call makes sampled diagnostic reproducible, keeps two runs identical data flapping alert alert. Sampling can miss rare issues. 0.1% keys zero-width space, 5,000-row sample 10-million-row table might include . Running full diagnostics periodically (weekly, upstream source changes) alongside sampled daily runs covers speed thoroughness.
code: # Simulate a large dataset set.seed(42) big_orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = sample(paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:5000)), 50000, replace = TRUE),   amount = round(runif(50000, 10, 500), 2),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  big_customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:6000)),   region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), 6000, replace = TRUE),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Full analysis system.time(report_full <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers, by = "customer_id")) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.19    0.03    0.26  # Sampled analysis system.time(report_sampled <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers,                                         by = "customer_id", sample = 5000)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.12    0.00    0.11 report_sampled$sampling #> $sampled #> [1] TRUE #>  #> $original_x_rows #> [1] 50000 #>  #> $original_y_rows #> [1] 6000 #>  #> $sample_size #> [1] 5000

text: realistic production workflow: nightly job loads order customer data, validates keys, repairs needed, joins cardinality enforcement, logs everything. three gates catch different failure modes. Gate 1 catches string-level problems repairs mechanical ones; run finds padded "C002 " trims , customer keeps name tier enriched output. Gate 2 halts one structural problem repair touch: unexpected many--many relationship, kind multiplies rows. Gate 3 catch-– left join returning fewer rows left table means something went wrong level key diagnostics see, upstream filter applied wrong object. Logging runs throughout set_log_file() called top, even run dies gate 3 leaves record join preceded death. Equally deliberate pattern check. never verifies customer_id right column join ; clean key wrong column passes every gate. enforces match-rate floor, orders customers missing reference table sail NA names – adding fourth gate last_report()$match_analysis$match_rate covers case unmatched keys count failures job hand. validates keys : corrupted amount column invisible every check , gates complement value-level validation tools rather replace . pattern stops scaling somewhere around dozen joins. point gate blocks become copy-paste boilerplate, thresholds belong config file, per-join logic wants function taking two tables, key, expected cardinality. Past , pipeline framework targets better skeleton, checks living inside node. log file also grows without bound daily schedule; naming date (sprintf("enrich-%s.log", Sys.Date())) keeps day’s audit trail separate bounded.
code: # ============================================================ # Nightly order enrichment pipeline # ============================================================  # --- Setup logging --- pipeline_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".log") set_log_file(pipeline_log, format = "text") #> ℹ Automatic logging enabled: C:\Users\GILLES~1\AppData\Local\Temp\Rtmp44YbEN\file82c88b665f5.log  # --- Load data (simulated) --- orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:6,   customer_id = c("C001", "C002 ", "C003", "C003", "C004", "C005"),   amount = c(150, 230, 89, 410, 320, 175),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C001", "C002", "C003", "C004", "C005", "C006"),   name = c("Acme Corp", "Globex", "Initech", "Umbrella", "Soylent", "Wonka"),   tier = c("gold", "silver", "gold", "bronze", "silver", "gold"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # --- Gate 1: key quality assertion --- keys_ok <- key_check(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)  if (!keys_ok) {   message("Key issues detected -- attempting repair")   repaired <- join_repair(     orders, customers,     by = "customer_id",     trim_whitespace = TRUE,     remove_invisible = TRUE   )   orders <- repaired$x   customers <- repaired$y } #> Key issues detected -- attempting repair #> ✔ Repaired 1 value(s)  # --- Gate 2: cardinality check --- card <- detect_cardinality(orders, customers, by = "customer_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "n:1" #> Left duplicates: 1 key(s) if (card == "n:m") {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Unexpected n:m cardinality in orders-customers join", call. = FALSE) }  # --- Join (with auto-logging via *_join_spy) --- enriched <- left_join_spy(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # --- Gate 3: row count sanity check --- # A left join should never lose rows from the left table if (nrow(enriched) < nrow(orders)) {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Row count decreased after left join -- possible data corruption",        call. = FALSE) }  # --- Output --- message(sprintf("Pipeline complete: %d enriched orders", nrow(enriched))) #> Pipeline complete: 6 enriched orders head(enriched) #>   customer_id order_id amount      name   tier #> 1        C001        1    150 Acme Corp   gold #> 2        C002        2    230    Globex silver #> 3        C003        3     89   Initech   gold #> 4        C003        4    410   Initech   gold #> 5        C004        5    320  Umbrella bronze #> 6        C005        6    175   Soylent silver  # --- Review the log --- if (file.exists(pipeline_log)) {   cat(readLines(pipeline_log), sep = "\n") } #>  #> Logged: 2026-06-13 00:54:09 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: customer_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 5 #>   Duplicated keys: 1 #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 6 #>   Duplicated keys: 0 #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 5 #>   Keys only in left: 0 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 100% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 6 #>   left_join: 6 #>   right_join: 7 #>   full_join: 7 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   duplicates: 1 #> ============================================================  # --- Cleanup --- set_log_file(NULL) #> ℹ Automatic logging disabled unlink(pipeline_log)

Setting warn = FALSE silences printing entirely, mode want inside scripts: return value plain logical, slots stopifnot() conditional logic. line like stopifnot(key_check(x, y, = "id", warn = FALSE)) turns key quality hard precondition: script halts join site instead producing silently wrong result.

title: Quick Start
text: Sometimes join already happened need understand result. Maybe join sits someone else’s code, maybe inputs gone memory output survived long pipeline run, maybe reviewing script row count quietly doubles two steps. Two functions cover direction: join_explain() reconstructs result size , join_diff() compares table post-join self.

title: Quick Start
text: join_explain() takes result, two input tables, join column, explains row count changed: output two parts. row-count section states sizes flags direction change. explanation section decomposes change causes: duplicate keys multiplying rows, unmatched keys dropped padded, NA keys never match. Usually table grows unexpectedly, answer handful keys duplicates one side. Passing type tells function join performed attributes dropped rows correctly. explanation also returned invisibly list, row counts, difference, per-side duplicate counts, matched/left-/right-key counts: result lost one row relative tickets one left key match: inner join dropped event 3. Capturing list lets pipeline assert decomposition, example failing run whenever left_only nonzero.
code: tickets <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   seat = c("A1", "B2", "B3", "C1"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  events <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Concert", "Play", "Opera"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- merge(tickets, events, by = "event_id") expl <- join_explain(result, tickets, events, by = "event_id", type = "inner") #>  #> ── Join Explanation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Row Counts ── #>  #> Left table (x): 4 rows #> Right table (y): 3 rows #> Result: 3 rows #> ℹ Result has 1 fewer rows than left table #>  #> ── Why the row count changed ── #>  #> ℹ Left table has 1 duplicate key(s) - each match creates multiple rows #> ℹ Inner join dropped 1 unmatched left rows expl$diff #> [1] -1 expl$left_only #> [1] 1

text: join_diff() compares data frame join show rows added, dropped, preserved: = TRUE nothing dropped: row id = 1 survives NA extra, id = 5 enters lookup side. diff reports one added row, one added column, one unique key join . argument optional; without comparison covers dimensions columns , join_diff() adds key analysis. second example highlights row addition duplicates rather new keys: row count grew three four id = 1 matched two rows lookup. new keys introduced; growth came entirely duplicate expansion, key analysis shows duplicate rows appearing none. join_diff() distinguishes two sources row addition (new keys vs. duplicate expansion). Like join_explain(), returns findings invisibly, including added removed column names:
code: before <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   value = c("a", "b", "c", "d"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(2, 3, 4, 5),   extra = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after <- merge(before, lookup, by = "id", all = TRUE) join_diff(before, after, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 4 rows x 2 columns #> After: 5 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: extra #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 4 #> Unique keys after: 5 before2 <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   value = c("a", "b", "c"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dup_lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   tag = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after2 <- merge(before2, dup_lookup, by = "id") d <- join_diff(before2, after2, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 3 rows x 2 columns #> After: 4 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: tag #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 3 #> Unique keys after: 3 #> Duplicate rows: "+2" d$columns_added #> [1] "tag"

text: *_join_spy() family wraps standard joins automatic diagnostics. print report joining return joined data frame: report, wrapper announces join, performs , compares actual row count prediction, flagging discrepancy. final check closes loop: mismatch predicted actual rows means prediction’s assumptions (usually around NA keys) hold, message says join site. four wrappers (left_join_spy, right_join_spy, inner_join_spy, full_join_spy) share interface. wrappers convenient exploratory work; production, might prefer calling join_spy() first inspecting report programmatically proceeding, join_strict() cardinality worth enforcing. wrappers also accept backend parameter ("base", "dplyr", "data.table") overrides automatic backend detection. Every wrapper attaches full report result attribute, diagnostics travel data: function several layers downstream can recover report data frame received check happened join, extra plumbing.
code: patients <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P03"),   age = c(34, 28, 45),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  labs <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P04"),   result = c(5.2, 3.8, 6.1),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- left_join_spy(patients, labs, by = "patient_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: patient_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 1 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ℹ 3 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'P03' ~ 'P01', 'P03' ~ 'P02', 'P03' ~ 'P04') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 2 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 4 #> ℹ Performing "left" join... #> ✔ Result: 3 rows (as expected) head(result) #>   patient_id age result #> 1        P01  34    5.2 #> 2        P02  28    3.8 #> 3        P03  45     NA rpt <- attr(result, "join_report") rpt$expected_rows$left #> [1] 3

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: public health researcher compiles data multiple sources systematic review. studies published supplementary tables PDF, copies table PDF viewer, pastes spreadsheet, cleans columns, reads CSV R. data looks perfect – every country name spelled correctly. half countries fail match reference population table. Printing PDF data shows nothing wrong: zero-width space “India” “France” occupies zero pixels. non-breaking space “Japan” renders like regular space U+00A0, U+0020 – trimws() always remove . merge reflects : Three six countries match. join_spy() detects invisible characters: Alongside encoding warning, near-match check reaches conclusion different direction: contaminated name sits one edit away clean counterpart reference table. Six matches. PDF copy-paste common source artifacts, web scraping, OCR output, legacy mainframe exports can produce . One useful debugging trick outside joinspy: nchar("India\u200B") returns 6, 5. requires already suspecting problem. watched code point typical entry route. non-breaking space comes typesetting: PDF layout engines HTML ( ) use keep words one line, clipboard preserves faithfully. Zero-width spaces inserted content-management systems word processors line-break hints. byte order mark arrives front files saved “UTF-8 BOM” Windows editors, fuses first field name first key CSV. survive visual inspection, disappear join_repair()’s invisible-character pass. Stripping right read.csv() keeps every downstream join .
code: # Simulating PDF copy-paste artifacts: # \u00A0 is non-breaking space, \u200B is zero-width space pdf_data <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India\u200B", "Germany",               "Japan\u00A0", "Canada", "France\u200B"),   prevalence = c(12.3, 8.7, 5.1, 3.9, 6.2, 4.8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  reference <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India", "Germany", "Japan",               "Canada", "France", "Italy"),   population_m = c(214, 1408, 84, 125, 38, 68, 59),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) pdf_data$country #> [1] "Brazil"  "India​"   "Germany" "Japan "  "Canada"  "France​" nrow(merge(pdf_data, reference, by = "country")) #> [1] 3 report <- join_spy(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") report #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: country #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 6 Unique keys: 6 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 7 Unique keys: 7 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 3 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 4 #> Match rate (left): "50%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'country' has encoding issues (invisible chars or mixed encoding) #> ℹ 3 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'India​' ~ 'India', 'Japan ' ~ 'Japan', 'France​' ~ 'France') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 3 #> left_join: 6 #> right_join: 7 #> full_join: 10 repaired <- join_repair(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) nrow(merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "country")) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: e-commerce pipeline joins transaction records product catalogue. pipeline ran cleanly months, match rates started drifting: 99% January, 97% February, 94% March. Nobody noticed finance flagged margin discrepancy April. code changed. new data entry clerk joined warehouse team December. canonical product code format "WDG-100" – uppercase prefix, dash, three-digit suffix. new clerk sometimes omitted dash, sometimes typed lowercase. warehouse system fuzzy matching internally, accepted codes ETL join’s exact comparison rejected. codes match , makes partial failures harder spot complete ones. report stacks several findings: case check catches "wdg-103", near-match check lines dashless codes catalogue entries one two edits away. scenario differs previous ones. join_repair() can fix case issue, insert missing dashes – requires domain knowledge code format. can dry run see join_repair() fix: applying mechanical fixes: case issues resolved, missing dashes remain. manual transformation handles : eight transactions match. join_repair() handles context-free transformations (trimming, case normalization, stripping invisible characters). Inserting dash "WDG102" requires knowing canonical format – fix come someone understands data. upstream story general five: two systems different matching strictness, connected people. warehouse software tolerated sloppy codes internal lookup fuzzy; ETL join exact; clerk way see difference. Keys typed humans drift like wherever entry goes unvalidated. rule rejecting anything fails ^[-Z]{3}-\d{3}$ warehouse boundary caught every malformed code December. drift also visible R side: match rate sliding 99% 94% three months measurable signal, logging report per pipeline run (vignette("production") covers log_report() set_log_file()) turns slide alert long finance notices margins.
code: # Product catalogue (canonical format) catalogue <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG-102",                    "WDG-103", "WDG-104", "WDG-105"),   product_name = c("Widget A", "Widget B", "Widget C",                    "Widget D", "Widget E", "Widget F"),   margin = c(0.35, 0.28, 0.42, 0.31, 0.39, 0.25),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Recent transactions (mix of old and new clerk entries) transactions <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG102",                    "wdg-103", "WDG-104", "wdg105",                    "WDG-100", "WDG103"),   quantity = c(5, 3, 7, 2, 4, 6, 1, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(transactions, catalogue, by = "product_code") report #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: product_code #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 8 Unique keys: 7 Duplicate keys: 1 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 6 Unique keys: 6 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 3 #> Keys only in left: 4 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "42.9%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left table has 1 duplicate key(s) affecting 2 rows - may cause row multiplication #> ! 1 key(s) would match if case-insensitive (e.g., 'wdg-103' vs 'WDG-103') #> ℹ 10 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'WDG102' ~ 'WDG-102', 'WDG103' ~ 'WDG-103', 'WDG102' ~ 'WDG-100') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 4 #> left_join: 8 #> right_join: 7 #> full_join: 11 join_repair(transactions, catalogue,             by = "product_code",             standardize_case = "upper",             dry_run = TRUE) #>  #> ── Repair Preview (Dry Run) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Left table (x) ── #>  #> ℹ product_code: upper case (2) repaired <- join_repair(transactions, catalogue,                         by = "product_code",                         standardize_case = "upper") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) # Manual fix: insert dash if missing in product codes matching the pattern fix_codes <- function(codes) {   gsub("^([A-Z]{3})(\\d)", "\\1-\\2", codes) } repaired$x$product_code <- fix_codes(repaired$x$product_code) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "product_code") nrow(result) #> [1] 8

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: Two government datasets need linked: regional economic indicators regional population estimates, keyed region year. year column numeric matches without trouble. region column whitespace problem affects records. compound key join, columns must match. year column fine everywhere, "Asia Pacific " trailing space match "Asia Pacific". Four rows instead six. North America Europe match; Asia Pacific , trailing space. join_spy() pinpoints column compound key problem: per-column breakdown shows year column matching fully region falls short, names region column lowest match rate. whitespace warning identifies exact values fault. Six rows. compound keys, string issue single column enough break match. columns key, places byte-level discrepancy can occur. Compound keys generalize everything vignette: column added one surface defects can land. Region--year panels, site--date field records, patient--visit tables: wherever observations keyed combination identifiers, component columns tend come different upstream systems different hygiene. aggregate match rate say component fault. per-column breakdown can, checking first rules candidate explanations bytes get inspected.
code: economics <- data.frame(   region = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific ",              "North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific "),   year = c(2022, 2022, 2022, 2023, 2023, 2023),   gdp_growth = c(2.1, 1.8, 4.2, 1.9, 0.9, 3.8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  population <- data.frame(   region = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific",              "North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"),   year = c(2022, 2022, 2022, 2023, 2023, 2023),   pop_millions = c(580, 450, 4300, 585, 448, 4350),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) merged <- merge(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) nrow(merged) #> [1] 4 report <- join_spy(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) report #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: region, year #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 6 Unique keys: 6 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 6 Unique keys: 6 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 4 #> Keys only in left: 2 #> Keys only in right: 2 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'region' has 1 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 1 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Asia Pacific ' ~ 'Asia Pacific') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Per-Column Breakdown ── #>  #> region: "66.7%" match rate (2/3) #> year: "100%" match rate (2/2) #> ℹ Lowest match rate: region #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 4 #> left_join: 6 #> right_join: 6 #> full_join: 8 repaired <- join_repair(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = c("region", "year")) nrow(result) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: five scenarios share three properties. data looks correct standard inspection tools – str(), summary(), print() render values identically. R returns fewer () rows without warning, key values genuinely differ byte level. fix mechanical cause known – trimming, lowercasing, stripping invisible Unicode one-line operations. join_spy() surfaces cause directly, especially useful data external sources, manual entry, PDF extraction, cross-system integrations. Worked way around, shared structure becomes checklist. question join post-mortem maps one call: join safe run? join_spy(x, y, ) gives full report: key summaries, match rates, detected issues, expected row counts four join types. want yes/gate script. key_check(x, y, ) prints short status invisibly returns logical; warn = FALSE makes silent. rows share key? key_duplicates(data, ) returns duplicated rows .n_duplicates count column. cleanup change? join_repair() dry_run = TRUE previews repairs without touching data. Fix mechanical problems. join_repair() trims whitespace, standardizes case, strips invisible characters, optionally converts empty strings NA. Show fix code. suggest_repairs(report) prints copy-pasteable snippets matched issues report found. join already ran row count wrong. join_explain(result, x, y, ) reconstructs rows went. Two versions table differ change unclear. join_diff(, , ) compares dimensions, columns, key statistics. must never happen silently . join_strict() performs join errors expectation like expect = "1:1" violated. split diagnosis repair deliberate. join_spy() never modifies data, join_repair() applies transformations correct regardless keys mean: trimming, casing, stripping invisible characters. Anything requires knowing data, like missing dashes Scenario 4, stays human decision, diagnostics pointing . wider catalogue join failures beyond strings (duplicate keys multiplying rows, NA keys, accidental cartesian products), see vignette("common-issues"). tour full API one worked example, see vignette("quickstart"). string-level defects collected ones resist inspection longest; one diagnostic call joining data generate covers every one .

text: package provides diagnostic functions organized three categories: join_spy() - Comprehensive pre-flight report key_check() - Quick key quality assessment key_duplicates() - Find duplicate keys join_explain() - Explain row count changes join_diff() - Compare /states left_join_spy() - left_join automatic reporting join_strict() - Fails cardinality constraints violated

title: CLAUDE.md
text: JoinReport S3 class - Returned join_spy() related functions: - x_summary - Summary left table keys - y_summary - Summary right table keys - match_analysis - /won’t match - issues - Detected problems (duplicates, whitespace, encoding, case) - expected_rows - Predicted row counts join type

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package detects common issues: 1. Duplicate keys - Keys appearing multiple times (causes row multiplication) 2. Trailing/leading whitespace - Invisible characters breaking matches 3. Case mismatches - “ABC” vs “abc” won’t match 4. Encoding issues - UTF-8 vs Latin-1, invisible Unicode characters 5. NA keys - Missing values join columns

title: joinspy 0.8.2
text: Logged reports now record duplicate-key counts. Text JSON logs previously left count blank serializer read mistyped field name (#1). Composite (multi-column) keys missing component now treated NA, matching joins handle . corrects NA counts, duplicate detection, row-count predictions composite keys (#3). Predicted row counts longer overflow NA large joins; counts kept doubles (#4). %||% imported rlang, package works R 4.1 4.3 (#2). Report printing key_check() treat issue text data, braces column names values longer interpreted cli (#5). suggest_repairs() generates valid code non-syntactic column names using [[ ]] indexing (#6).

title: joinspy 0.8.2
text: Key resolution, composite-key construction, column checks, cardinality classification now share single-source helpers (#7). Errors use cli consistent, classed messages. JSON logging escapes backslashes control characters.
